content_html:
MGT10009 Contemporary Management Principles
Lecture 12b Creating & Leading Change
Dr David Qian dqian@swin.edu.au
Peter Moran pmoran@swin.edu.au

Learning Objectives
18-1 Discuss what it takes to be world class.
18-2 Describe how to manage and lead change effectively.
18-3 Describe strategies for creating a successful future.
‹#›

Management in Action Shell Tries to Manage Environmental Change
As the world grapples with global climate change, many in private industry are working to provide solutions. Because we live in an energy-dependent world and much of that energy still comes from burning fossil fuels, the oil industry is under intense scrutiny.
Many of the major oil companies, have been slow to acknowledge global climate change and even slower to mitigate carbon emissions. However, some progress is afoot.
‹#›

Achieving Excellence
Being world class requires:
Applying the best and latest knowledge and ideas.
Having the ability to operate at the highest standards of any place anywhere.
Great companies …
Have strong core values.
Are driven by stretch goals.
Change continuously.
Focus on beating themselves, not on beating the competition.
‹#›

Exhibit 18.1 Core Ideologies in Built-to-Last Companies
3M:
Innovation—“Thou shalt not kill a new product idea.”
Absolute integrity.
Respect for individual initiative and personal growth.
Tolerance for honest mistakes.
Product quality and reliability.
“Our real business is solving problems.”
Sony:
To experience the sheer joy that comes from the advancement, application, and innovation of technology that benefits the general public.
To elevate the Japanese culture and national status.
Being pioneers—not following others, but doing the impossible.
Respecting and encouraging each individual’s ability and creativity.
‹#›

Exhibit 18.1 Core Ideologies in Built-to-Last Companies
Walmart:
“We exist to provide value to our customers”—to make their lives better via lower prices and greater selection; all else is secondary.
Swim upstream, buck conventional wisdom.
Be in partnership with employees.
Work with passion, commitment, and enthusiasm.
Run lean.
Pursue ever-higher goals.
Disney:
No cynicism allowed.
Fanatical attention to consistency and detail.
Continuous progress via creativity, dreams, and imagination.
Fanatical control and preservation of Disney’s “magic” image.
“To bring happiness to millions” and to celebrate, nurture, and promulgate “wholesome American values.”.
SOURCE: James C. Collins and Jerry I. Porras, Built to Last (New York: HarperCollins, 19 97).
‹#›

The Tyranny of the Or
The belief that things must be either A or B and cannot be both; that only one goal and not another can be attained
Examples:
You must choose either change or stability.
Be conservative or bold.
Have control and consistency or creative freedom.
Do well in the short term or invest for the future.
Plan methodically or be opportunistic.
Create shareholder wealth or do good for the world.
Be pragmatic or idealistic.
‹#›

The Genius of the And
Ability to achieve multiple objectives simultaneously
Organizational ambidexterity.
Ability to achieve multiple objectives simultaneously.
Examples:
Purpose beyond profit and pragmatic pursuit of profit.
Relatively fixed core values and vigorous change and movement.
Conservatism with the core values and bold business moves.
Clear vision and direction and experimentation.
Stretch goals and incremental progress.
Control based on values and operational freedom.
Long-term thinking and investment and demand for short-term results.
Visionary, futuristic thinking and daily, nuts-and-bolts execution.
‹#›

Sustaining Greatness
Researchers identified four key factors that lead to sustained superior performance.
Strategy.
Execution.
Culture.
Structure.
‹#›

Organization Development (O D)
The systemwide application of behavioral science knowledge to develop, improve, and reinforce the strategies, structures, and processes that lead to organizational effectiveness
Broad approach to changing people, teams, work units.
Not directly concerned with economic, financial or technical aspects of the organization.
Introduces changes for the long term.
‹#›

Exhibit 18.2 Basic Types of O D Interventions
Intervention
Goals
Strategic
Helping organizations conduct mergers and acquisitions, change their strategies, and develop alliances
Techno-structural
Relating to organization structure and design, employee involvement, and work design
Human process
Improving conflict resolution, team building, communication, and leadership
Human resource management
Attracting good people, setting goals, and appraising and rewarding performance
SOURCE: T. Cummings and C. Worley, Organization Development and Change, 10th ed. (Stamford, CT: Cengage , 2015).
‹#›

Managing Change
Shared leadership is crucial to the success of most change efforts.
People must be not just supporters of change, but also implementers.
It is essential to motivate people to keep changing in response to new business challenges.
‹#›

Exhibit 18.3 Reasons for Resistance to Change
Inertia
Timing
Surprise
Peer pressure
Self-interest
Misunderstanding
Different assessments
Management tactics
‹#›

A General Model for Managing Resistance
Unfreezing: realizing current practices are inappropriate and new behavior is necessary.
Performance gap.
Moving: instituting the change
Force-field analysis.
Refreezing: strengthening the new behaviors that support change
‹#›

Exhibit 18.4 Motivating People to Change
Unfreezing
Breaking from the old ways of doing things.
Moving
Instituting the change.
Refreezing
Reinforcing and supporting the new ways.
‹#›

Enlisting Cooperation
Developing true support is better than simply driving a program forward:
Education and communication.
Participation and involvement.
Facilitation and support.
Negotiation and rewards.
Manipulation and co-potation.
Explicit and implicit coercion.
‹#›

Management in Action Balancing the Energy Needs of Today and Tomorrow
Even as fossil-fuel companies begin to pivot to address consumers’ and investors’ concerns about climate change, they have not abandoned the fossil-fuel business. The world’s largest oil companies recently spent on average just 1 percent of their annual budgets on clean energy. Still, there’s a general acknowledgment that the world’s energy sources can and will change. The questions that each oil company has to answer are, how fast will change occur and how can they best manage it?
If you were the C E O of a major oil firm, how would you respond to pressures to become a more environmentally friendly company? What would your energy strategy be?
If you were president of a privately-owned oil business, how might your strategies differ?
‹#›

Harmonizing Multiple Changes
Questions to ask before embarking on a change:
What is the evidence that the approach really can produce positive results?
Is the approach relevant to your company’s strategies and priorities?
Can you assess the costs and potential benefits?
Does it really help people add value through their work?
Does it help the company focus better on customers and the things they value?
‹#›

Exhibit 18.6 Leading Change
SOURCE: John P. Kotter, Leading Change (Boston: Harvard Business School Publishing, 19 96).
‹#›

Exhibit 18.7 Sources of Complacency
SOURCE: John P. Kotter, Leading Change (Boston: Harvard Business School Publishing, 19 96).
‹#›

Shaping the Future
Reactive change:
A response that occurs under pressure; problem-driven change.
Proactive change:
A response that is initiated before a performance gap has occurred.
‹#›

Creating the Future
Adapters:
Companies that take the current industry structure and its evolution as givens and choose where to compete.
Shapers:
Companies that try to change the structure of their industries, creating a future competitive landscape of their own design.
‹#›

SPOTLIGHT ON… Leveraging A I to Build a Better Future
An overarching goal of any social enterprise is to do good—for people, the environment, even the world. But certain technologies and specifically artificial intelligence (A I) enter our lives, and social entrepreneurs want to be mindful of how to deploy them in ways that advance social justice while mitigating its potential pitfalls.
What is your take on A I? Is it a useful tool for the future of social enterprises? Why or why not?
If you were the founder of Lemonade, what risks would you need to manage as you used A I?
‹#›

Exhibit 18.8 Opportunity Is Finding Ways to Meet Customers’ Needs
Access the text alternative for slide image
‹#›

Which Should You and Your Firm Do?
Preserve old advantages or create new advantages?
Lock in old markets or create new markets?
Take the path of greatest familiarity or the path of greatest opportunity?
Be only a benchmarker or a pathbreaker?
Place priority on short-term financial returns or on making a real, long-term impact?
Do only what seems doable or what is difficult and worthwhile?
Change what is or create what isn’t?
Look to the past or live for the future?
‹#›

Shaping Your Own Future
Create the future you want for yourself.
Set high personal standards.
Take charge of your own development.
Become indispensable to your organization.
Maintain your options.
Develop resilience.
‹#›

Exhibit 18.9 Critical Skills You’ll Need for the Future
Exhibit 18.9
Critical Skills You’ll Need for the Future
Creativity
Emotional intelligence
Analytical (critical) thinking
Active learning with a growth mindset
Judgment and decision making
Interpersonal communication skills
Leadership skills
Diversity and cultural intelligence
Technology skills
Embracing change
SOURCE: B. Marr, “The 10 Vital Skills You Will Need for the Future of Work,” Forbes, April 29, 2019, www.forbes.com.
‹#›

Learning and Leading
Continuous learning is a vital route to renewable advantage.
Take risks.
Ask for opinions.
Be open to new ideas.
‹#›

Exhibit 18.10 Learning Cycle: Explore, Discover, Act
SOURCE: Adapted from George Binney, Collin Williams, Leaning into the Future: Changing the Way People Change Organizations (Boston: Nicholas Brealey Publishing, 19 97).
‹#›

Exhibit 18.11 Level 5 Hierarchy
SOURCE: Jim Collins, Good to Great (New York: HarperCollins, 2001).
Access the text alternative for slide image
‹#›

A Collaborative, Sustainable Future?
As you lead and learn into the future:
Think long term, along with handling the immediate demands you must face.
Consider collaboration as a key to sustained success.
Business and tomorrow’s leaders in every sector help determine the world’s future.
‹#›

Management in Action Siemens: Reinventing Itself to Manage Change
Siemens, a global technology leader, is an example of a firm that is trying to reinvent itself in order to reinvent the future.
Like any organization, Siemens has to manage change by considering short-term versus long-term goals, as well as its internal and external stakeholders. Do you think Siemens’s decision to drastically downsize and streamline its operations was the right move? Why or why not?
Busch has staked the future of Siemens on his belief that can compete as a technology company. Check in on how Siemens is doing, as well as global energy trends. Is their vision proving correct, or does it seem to have been off somehow?
‹#›

In Review
Discuss what it takes to be world class.
Describe how to manage and lead change effectively.
Describe strategies for creating a successful future.
‹#›
